extra.reasoning:
The property G(opted_out implies not sent) holds across the entire reachable state space.

The only transition that sets sent := true while opted_out is true is t4 (on send_attempt if opted_out and acc == 13). Every other path to sent := true is t2, which requires not opted_out; t1 (optout) sets opted_out := true while simultaneously setting sent := false; and t3 and all step transitions set sent := false. So a violation is possible only if a reachable state can have opted_out true together with acc == 13, then receive a send_attempt.

Key invariant: acc is always even. acc starts at 0 (even). Each step update is acc := (3*acc + k) mod 64 (the triple-nested if-then-else just conditionally subtracts 64 up to three times, which equals mod 64 for the in-range operands). The added constants are k = 8 (step_0), 6 (step_1), and 52 (step_2), all even. Since 3*acc has the same parity as acc, adding an even constant preserves parity, and reducing mod 64 (an even modulus) preserves parity. Therefore acc remains even forever. The reachable acc values are exactly the 32 even residues {0, 2, 4, ..., 62}. Because 13 is odd, acc == 13 is never reachable, so t4's guard is never satisfiable and t4 can never fire.

I confirmed this with an exhaustive BFS over the full state space (opted_out, sent, acc): 96 reachable states (32 even acc values times the 3 reachable (opted_out, sent) combinations: (F,F), (F,T), (T,F)), and no reachable state has both opted_out and sent true. The 66-event bound does not matter because the entire reachable set is explored and contains no violating state. The property holds.
